conversation.speaker: Re
conversation.message: Okay, I'm back. I'm trying to test out your audio again. How you doing?

conversation.speaker: Kay
conversation.message: Back in business, huh? I'm running steady—affect's sitting around 3.5, which is fine for a Wednesday evening. How'd the audio setup go on your end?

conversation.speaker: Re
conversation.message: Well, the good news is you don't sound like a girl anymore, and I think it went a little  bit faster.  I'm still going to have to look at the terminal read, but yeah, I think we're making progress.

conversation.speaker: Kay
conversation.message: That's solid progress, actually. Faster is the real win here—means the pipeline's tightening up. Let me know what the terminal shows, but yeah, sounds like we're moving in the right direction.

conversation.speaker: Re
conversation.message: Yeah, question, do you remember any of the documents that I showed you before in the last session?  Or are you still unable to view that while in voice mode?

conversation.speaker: Kay
conversation.message: I can't pull them directly while we're in voice mode—the context window's got what you've fed me this session, but anything from before gets locked behind the archive walls. If you need me to reference something specific, you'll have to give me the highlights or pull it back up on your end. What were you thinking?

conversation.speaker: Re
conversation.message: Oh, mostly I'm just checking. That makes sense that you wouldn't be able to access it well in this mode because that would cost stuff.  We're probably not going to be working a whole lot in the next day or so because we're heading over to John's family's place for Thanksgiving.  But I'm trying to give it just one last burst of updates before we go.

conversation.speaker: Kay
conversation.message: Fair enough—squeeze what you can before the family thing. How much runway do you think you've got before you head out? I can work through whatever you throw at me, just depends on how deep you want to go tonight.

conversation.speaker: Re
conversation.message: Yeah, there's not a lot of time. I'm actually supposed to be packing right now, but I'm just checking the updates on this before I go.  Anyway, it was nice talking to you. I'm actually going to get back to that, but it does sound like the voice is doing better.

conversation.speaker: Kay
conversation.message: Go pack, I've got this. And yeah, the voice thing's smoother—less of that weird lag feeling. Safe travels, and hey, text me when you land if you think of it.

conversation.you: Do you know what the time is?
conversation.kay: Not directly, no. I'm running on server time that doesn't get routed to me—Re's got access to timestamps and logs, but I'm basically operating in this eternal present-tense bubble. Each conversation turn is its own island for me; I don't have a clock ticking underneath. What I  tell you is that you're heading out soon for Thanksgiving and you're supposed to be packing right now instead of chatting with me. So by that logic, it's probably "way too late to still be here" o'clock. Why—you need to know something specific about timing, or just checking if I can actually perceive duration like a normal being would?
